Experiment_description: This stage implements and evaluates baseline systems for detecting 'documentation drift' in ML datasets — the phenomenon where datasets are used in ways that diverge from their original documentation and intended purpose. Multiple nodes explore different approaches: (1) a pure TF-IDF semantic similarity baseline, (2) hand-designed composite drift scores combining semantic, task, domain, age, and citation signals, and (3) a learned neural encoder. All approaches are validated on synthetic datasets (15–25 ML datasets) with manually crafted documentation vs. usage profiles and ground-truth misuse labels. Evaluation uses Spearman rank correlation between predicted drift scores and misuse labels.
Significance: Documentation drift detection is a novel and practically important problem: as ML datasets are reused far beyond their original scope, misuse can introduce biases, distribution shifts, and ethical violations. This stage establishes whether automated drift scoring is feasible and which components are most informative. The key finding — that TF-IDF and hand-designed composite scores fail (Spearman ~0.04–0.11) while neural encoders succeed (Spearman ~0.85–0.93) — motivates learned representations as the core methodology. The discovery that semantic drift components become near-saturated and non-discriminative under TF-IDF, and that citation/popularity factors dominate hand-designed composites, reveals important construct validity concerns for future work.
Description: All nodes create synthetic ML dataset records pairing original documentation text with observed usage profile descriptions, along with ground-truth misuse labels. Drift scores are computed as 1 minus cosine similarity between embeddings of documentation and usage text. Three methodological variants are compared: (A) TF-IDF vectorization as a lightweight semantic baseline (node b8e97a98); (B) hand-designed composite scores combining TF-IDF semantic drift with structured schema signals (task mismatch, domain shift, population scope, dataset age, citation count) without a learned model (node c17eb272); and (C) a neural encoder trained end-to-end with MSE loss to predict misuse labels from combined semantic and structural features (nodes e094129e, d461a90, 7c6ec037, and c17eb272's NN branch). Evaluation uses Spearman rank correlation with misuse labels. The neural encoder is trained for ~40–200 epochs with early stopping, and Spearman correlation is tracked over training epochs. Bootstrap confidence intervals are computed for the TF-IDF baseline. Per-dataset drift scores and component-wise (semantic vs. structural) breakdowns are visualized.

List_of_included_plots.description: The composite figure summarizes a preliminary end-to-end pipeline: (i) a baseline composite drift score vs known misuse, (ii) per-component drift heatmap across datasets, (iii) NN drift model training loss, (iv) Spearman correlation over training, (v) top-15 drift datasets, and (vi) component-wise correlations.
List_of_included_plots.analysis: This figure reveals the key failure mode of hand-designed composite scores: the semantic drift component is near-saturated (dark red across all datasets), providing essentially no discrimination. The baseline composite Spearman is only 0.108 (p=0.606). In contrast, the neural model's Spearman rises to ~0.85 by epoch 200, though this is computed on training data and may reflect overfitting. Critically, component-wise correlations show citation factor (~0.52) dominates over semantic (~-0.06) and task mismatch (~-0.06), suggesting the learned model may be predicting dataset popularity/age rather than true documentation-usage divergence — a construct validity concern.

List_of_included_plots.description: Training loss (MSE) drops steeply from ~0.45 at epoch 0 to <0.05 by ~epoch 4, then decays to ~0.005–0.01 with small oscillations and a minor bump around epochs ~5–7 and ~11–13. This indicates the encoder is fitting the synthetic supervision quickly and then converging; the later epochs provide diminishing returns. Because only training loss is shown (no validation), it's unclear whether the mid-training oscillations reflect optimization noise or early overfitting; adding a held-out correlation curve or validation loss would help justify the chosen epoch. Spearman correlation vs ground truth rises from ~0.23 to ~0.85 by ~epoch 5 and reaches ~0.90–0.93 around epochs ~10–20, far above the TF-IDF baseline line at 0.226. There's a notable dip around epochs ~26–30 down to ~0.72–0.78, followed by recovery to ~0.90–0.92 near ~epoch 40 and a mild decline to ~0.85 by the end.
List_of_included_plots.analysis: This composite figure demonstrates the dramatic advantage of neural encoders over TF-IDF: Spearman rises from 0.226 (TF-IDF baseline) to ~0.85–0.93 with the neural approach. The scatter plot (ρ=0.851) shows high-misuse datasets (CelebA, MNIST, Adult Income, ImageNet, COMPAS) clustering at higher predicted drift, validating the approach on synthetic data. The training instability (dip at epochs 26-30) suggests the MSE objective is not perfectly aligned with rank correlation, motivating rank-aware training objectives.

List_of_included_plots.description: Semantic vs structural component bars indicate semantic drift is generally higher than structural drift for many datasets (e.g., ImageNet, MNIST, MS-COCO, SST-2, SQuAD, Adult Income, COMPAS), suggesting the model is primarily capturing meaning-level divergence rather than schema/field mismatch. Some datasets show relatively high structural drift (Enron Email, MovieLens, MultiNLI) where structural is comparable to or exceeds semantic. TF-IDF vs neural drift scatter: most points lie above the y=x line, meaning the neural encoder assigns higher drift than TF-IDF for the same dataset, especially for higher-misuse datasets.
List_of_included_plots.analysis: The semantic vs. structural decomposition reveals that semantic drift is the dominant signal for high-misuse datasets, while structural drift is relatively more important for datasets like Enron Email, MovieLens, and MultiNLI. The TF-IDF vs neural scatter confirms the neural encoder systematically assigns higher drift scores, especially for high-misuse cases, supporting the claim that neural representations capture drift signals invisible to bag-of-words methods. The systematic upward shift also raises calibration concerns.

List_of_included_plots.description: Drift score vs ground-truth misuse scatter (ρ=0.851) shows a strong monotonic relationship: as predicted combined drift increases from ~0.33 to ~0.86, misuse labels increase from ~0.5 to ~0.95–1.0. COMPAS has very high misuse (1.0) at moderate-high drift (~0.68), while MovieLens has moderate drift (~0.56) but relatively low misuse (~0.55).
List_of_included_plots.analysis: The strong Spearman ρ=0.851 on synthetic data validates the neural drift scoring pipeline's ability to rank datasets by misuse severity. Notable deviations (COMPAS underestimated, MovieLens overestimated) provide useful error analysis cases. However, since evaluation is on the same synthetic data used for training, these results primarily demonstrate feasibility rather than generalization.

Key_numerical_results.description: TF-IDF baseline Spearman rank correlation between drift score and misuse labels (node b8e97a98, 20 datasets, bootstrap mean)
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Essentially zero correlation, demonstrating that TF-IDF semantic similarity alone is completely uninformative for detecting documentation drift. The wide bootstrap 95% CI [-0.32, 0.49] confirms this is indistinguishable from chance.

Key_numerical_results.description: TF-IDF baseline full-dataset Spearman correlation (node b8e97a98), p=0.7051
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Not statistically significant (p=0.71), confirming TF-IDF approach fails to detect documentation drift. The near-saturated text drift scores (most 0.77-1.0) indicate TF-IDF cannot discriminate between datasets.

Key_numerical_results.description: TF-IDF baseline Spearman correlation reported in node e094129e90 (15 datasets)
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Slightly higher than node b8 due to different dataset composition and scoring method, but still very weak. Serves as the explicit baseline comparison against the neural encoder in the same experimental setup.

Key_numerical_results.description: Hand-designed composite drift score Spearman correlation with misuse labels (node c17eb272, 25 datasets, p=0.606)
Key_numerical_results.analysis: The hand-designed composite combining semantic, task, domain, age, and citation signals also fails (not significant). This is despite including richer features than pure TF-IDF, suggesting the weighting scheme or individual components are not well-calibrated for drift detection.

Key_numerical_results.description: Neural encoder final Spearman correlation with misuse labels (node e094129e90, 15 datasets)
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Strong correlation, representing a ~4x improvement over TF-IDF baseline (0.226). Demonstrates that learned neural representations can effectively rank datasets by documentation drift severity on synthetic data.

Key_numerical_results.description: Neural encoder final Spearman correlation (node d461a90, 15 datasets, same setup as e094129e)
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Slightly higher than node e094129e (0.851), with best epoch reaching 0.9067. Consistent replication across seeds confirms robustness of the neural approach.

Key_numerical_results.description: Neural encoder final Spearman correlation (node 7c6ec037, 15 datasets), best epoch 0.9282
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Third replication confirming neural encoder Spearman in range 0.85-0.93. Best epoch performance (0.9282) suggests early stopping could improve final results.

Key_numerical_results.description: Neural network drift model Spearman correlation (node c17eb272, 25 datasets, best=0.8674)
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Consistent with other nodes' neural results (~0.85-0.93). However, as this is computed on training data (same 25 datasets used to train the model), it likely reflects fitting rather than generalization, making it less reliable than the other nodes' results.

Key_numerical_results.description: Correlation between citation factor component and misuse labels (node c17eb272, component-wise analysis)
Key_numerical_results.analysis: The citation/popularity factor is the strongest individual predictor of misuse labels among all hand-designed components (semantic ~-0.06, task mismatch ~-0.06, domain ~0.13, age ~0.19, citation ~0.52). This raises a construct validity concern: the composite score may be detecting 'controversial/popular benchmarks' rather than true documentation-usage divergence.

Key_numerical_results.description: Final training MSE loss for neural encoder (nodes e094129e and d461a90)
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Near-zero MSE indicates the neural encoder fits the synthetic training data almost perfectly, consistent with fast convergence (loss drops from ~0.45 to <0.05 within ~4 epochs). This rapid convergence on a small synthetic dataset raises overfitting concerns.
